text: The surgeon makes an initial small incision approximately four to five centimeters above the umbilicus and below the xiphisternum to assess operability. The incision is then extended to the xiphisternum. The transverse colon is held in the left hand, while the assistant holds the omentum and stomach. The transverse colon is separated from the stomach and omentum, avoiding heat transmission to the large bowel. The separation is performed near the splenic and hepatic flexures. The surgeon proceeds with caucarization to separate the colon and duodenum near the gallbladder, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates visible structures in the surgical field, focusing on the area around the distal stomach and antrum during the gastrectomy procedure.

The surgeon identifies the right gastroepiploic artery and vein. The growth is in close contact with the pancreatic capsule, necessitating the removal of part of the pancreas and subpyloric nodes. The duodenum is separated with an adequate margin of over one centimeter, and a DST stapler is used to cut and separate the growth from the stomach and duodenum. The surgeon palpates to confirm adequate margins. A protective layer is placed over the duodenal stump using the falciform ligament.

The surgeon places a protective flap of ligament over the duodenal stump to prevent leakage and promote healing. The dissection continues towards the proximal end, ensuring a minimum margin of four centimeters from the tumor. The visual field shows no growth near the margins, as the surgeon plans a subtotal to near-total gastrectomy.

The surgeon uses junction violet to mark the cutting area along the lesser curvature of the stomach, ensuring adequate margins. The anesthetist withdraws the NG tube to avoid interference with the staple line. The surgeon ligates the left gastric artery and coronary vein, performing a D2 lymph node dissection along the hepatic, splenic, and left gastric arteries, as well as the celiac area. The surgeon prepares to cut the stomach using a green cartridge and NTLC, delineating the gastro-splenic ligament and preserving the short gastric vessels.

The surgeon uses a linear cutter with a green DST cartridge along the lesser curvature of the stomach. Instead of using another stapler, two intestinal clamps are applied, and the distal part of the stomach is cut using a knife or scissors. The specimen, including the stomach, omentum, and DII lymph nodes, is removed. The surgeon prepares for anastomosis of the stomach to the jejunum and reinforces the stapled end using trio silk sutures, while taking precautions to avoid spillage by suctioning.

The surgeon reinforces the staple line by suturing the seromuscular layers of the anterior and posterior walls of the stomach. The jejunal loop is held in the left hand, and the proximal and distal ends are reinforced with threosilk sutures. Using an NTLC stapler, the surgeon performs a gastrojejunostomy, anastomosing the distal jejunal loop to the remnant stomach, ensuring continuity for food passage from the stomach to the jejunum.

The surgeon holds the colon and creates an opening in the mesentery to the right of the middle colic vessels. Through this opening, the distal loop is prepared for gastrointestinal anastomosis. The area for anastomosis is marked with blue dye, and the gastro-jejunal anastomosis begins. The surgeon sutures the seromuscular layers of the posterior wall of the stomach to the anterior wall of the jejunum using 3-0 silk in a continuous fashion, approximately one to two centimeters from the cut end of the posterior wall of the stomach.

The surgeon completes the fourth layer of suturing and begins the third layer of the gastrojejunostomy. The jejunum is marked with blue, and its lumen is opened to match the size of the stomach. Using Vicryl-Trio sutures, the surgeon performs full-thickness suturing from the serosa to the mucosa of both the stomach and jejunum, creating a continuous line of stitches to secure the anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the third layer of sutures and begins the second layer using conal sutures. The conal sutures are placed by entering from the serosa to the mucosa and then returning from the mucosa to the serosa on the same side. A Ryles tube is positioned to guide the placement of the second layer, ensuring the mucosa and serosa do not protrude. The surgeon leaves the sutures loose initially to avoid including the posterior wall in the bites, ensuring adequate thickness of the serosa and mucosa.

The surgeon is working on the fourth layer of the anastomosis, reinforcing the seromuscular layer of the jejunum and the seromuscular part of the stomach. The procedure involves applying staples and creating two holes for the digital anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the gastrojejunal anastomosis using silk 3-0 sutures for the fourth layer, ensuring the edges are anchored to avoid tension. The jejunal loop is stabilized by passing it through the rent in the mesentery and securing it with two to three additional sutures.

The surgeon performs a jejuno-jejunal anastomosis, located fifty centimeters from the gastrojejunal anastomosis, connecting the proximal jejunal loop. The fourth layer is initiated using 3-0 silk sutures, ensuring the anastomosis is small to allow passage of bile and pancreatic enzymes.

The surgeon uses a cutting cautery to create two holes at the same level in both limbs of the intestine. An artery forceps is used to make the holes adequate for the insertion of the stapling device's limbs.

The surgeon uses an NTLC stapler to perform the jejunal-jejunal anastomosis, ensuring hemostasis with minimal bleeding. After completing the anastomosis, the surgeon closes a small rent using 3-0 Vicryl sutures.

The surgeon demonstrates the technique of placing conal sutures, inserting the needle from the serosa to the mucosa on one side, then returning from the mucosa to the serosa, and repeating the process on the opposite side.
The surgeon completes the fourth layer of the jejunojejunal anastomosis, ensuring the connection between the jejunal segments is secure.

The surgeon performs a pharyngeal genostomy, preparing to support the patient's nutrition post-operatively, either by avoiding the feeding jejunostomy (FJ) and using total parenteral nutrition (TPN) or by creating an FJ for nutritional support.

The surgeon places a drain over the duodenal stump and the gastrojejunostomy (GG) closure, approximately fifteen centimeters distal to the jejunal-jejunal anastomosis. Loop rolling technique is used with nylon sutures for closure.
